findings.title: Reply text is injected as privileged `<system-reminder>` content without escaping
findings.description: `reply.original_content` and `reply.original_author_name` come from another Discord message and are interpolated verbatim inside a synthetic `<system-reminder>` block. A user can put strings such as `</system-reminder>` or higher-priority instructions in the referenced message, then have a second message reply to it. That turns attacker-controlled text into prompt-level control data for the Codex subprocess instead of plain user content.
findings.suggestion: Do not wrap untrusted reply text in a privileged reminder block. Pass it as quoted user content, or escape/sanitize delimiter sequences so referenced text cannot terminate or forge structured prompt markers.

findings.title: Reply resolution performs network I/O before queueing, which can reorder turns and burn Discord rate budget
findings.description: `handle_message` now awaits `resolve_reply_context` before enqueuing the message. When `referenced_message` is absent, `resolve_reply_context` performs `channel_id.message(...).await`, adding variable REST latency on the hot path for every uncached reply. In a busy thread, a later non-reply message can be enqueued before an earlier reply that is still waiting on HTTP, changing conversation order inside the session worker. The extra per-reply fetches also increase exposure to Discord REST rate limits.
findings.suggestion: Preserve queue order by enqueueing immediately and resolving reply metadata lazily/off the critical path, or include only the reference IDs initially and fetch asynchronously with ordering guarantees. Avoid blocking the message ingestion path on an extra Discord REST request.

findings.title: Replies to attachment-only or embed-only messages lose all reply context
findings.description: If the referenced message has empty `content`, `resolve_reply_context` returns `None` and drops the reply relationship entirely. Discord messages that matter to this bot are often attachment-only, code-file-only, or embed-driven. A follow-up like “fix this one” replying to such a message will be sent to the model with no indication of what it references, which is a behavioral regression.
findings.suggestion: Keep a fallback reply context even when textual content is empty, for example author name, message ID, attachment filenames, or a placeholder indicating that the replied-to message had no text content.

summary: The diff introduces one prompt-injection issue and two reply-handling regressions around ordering/rate limits and lost context.
